name: Swamp White Oak
summary: The most common riparian oak in NEPA — slow but reliable bottomland canopy tree. Adds a 6th oak species to the planting and brings Quercus (Tallamy keystone genus #1 at ~500 Lepidoptera species) to wet bottomland sites where chestnut and red oak won't thrive.
notes: Acorns feed deer, turkey, squirrel, bear, and wood ducks. White underside of leaf gives the species its name (bicolor). Long-lived (200+ years). Tolerates seasonal flooding and poorly-drained soils where most oaks fail. White oak group — not vulnerable to oak wilt as severely as red oak group.

planting.aspect: Seasonal wet hollows; bottomland; floodplain margins
planting.light: Full sun preferred; tolerates partial shade as sapling
planting.moisture: Wet to mesic; tolerates seasonal flooding
planting.soil: Acidic to neutral; clay-tolerant; poorly-drained OK
planting.site_notes: Plant in seasonally wet bottomland where regular oaks struggle. Standard bare-root from Wayne Conservation District. Tube protection (heat-tolerant); do not prune April–July (oak wilt risk).
